designs.name: VOID WORKBENCH — Manufacturing Inside the Mind
designs.oneLineConcept: The brain never docks and never shrinks — it keeps voyaging FULLSCREEN as the living backdrop, and the editor + preview rise as two translucent glass surfaces floating INSIDE its infinite space, with the HUD collapsed to a single calm voyaging status line.
designs.brainPresentation: UNCHANGED. The brain stays exactly as the home voyage: `.superbrain-experience` keeps `position:fixed; inset:0`, full viewport, native resolution, perpetual CameraDrift (z≈7.5 hero framing, ±0.12 dolly, 60s orbit, pointer parallax) — never frozen, never cropped. CRITICAL DECISION: in manufacturing I add NO transform/sized box to `.sb-brain-stage` (I delete the current 42vh dock rule). That means there is NO containing-block dock, so the fixed-FOV camera-crop trap (thin-band) and the side-console 3D-anchor clipping simply never occur — the brain renders at its canon framing. The workbench floats ON TOP of it (z-index above the scene, below the thin HUD line). To keep the brain readable behind glass and keep it the lead, the manufacturing class adds a product-only radial vignette/scrim *behind the workbench glass only* (a `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-void-veil` element I own, NOT a brain edit) so the editor text has contrast while the brain still drifts visibly around the panels' edges and through their translucency. The mind literally never stops travelling and never gets smaller — you work inside its space.
designs.hudHandling: Goal: collapse the full-screen HUD to ONE calm voyaging status line at the top, since the workbench now occupies the center where consoles/command-bar/terminal used to live. All overrides are product-only CSS scoped under `.sb-shell--manufacture` in a new `manufacturing.css` (loaded after superbrain.css, UNLAYERED so it beats the @layer components rules). KEEP + REPURPOSE: `.topbar` (superbrain.css:238) — kept as the thin voyaging status line. It's absolute-to-`.superbrain-experience` (full viewport here, so it spans the page top correctly, no 100vw issue). Override to thin it: `.sb-shell--manufacture .topbar{height:46px}`; `.sb-shell--manufacture .build-tag{display:none}` (lab drops it <620px); collapse the center summary by hiding the heaviest spans the lab already proves safe (`.system-summary .topbar-divider` trimming + hiding SURFACE/SOUND spans as the <1100px/<900px rules do). KEEP the live `.status-dot`, LATENCY, AUTONOMY readouts and the `.fidelity-button` sovereignty levers (FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE reachable). KEEP `.secure-button` (superbrain.css:355) Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER in that same top line — it rides the topbar's right grid cell, sacred trust posture stays visible. KEEP `.command-bar` (superbrain.css:1212) but re-anchor it as the page's global directive line and KILL the 100vw escape: `.sb-shell--manufacture .command-bar{position:fixed; bottom:18px; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); width:min(680px, calc(100% - 360px))}` — `100%` of the viewport (not 100vw, no 56px viewport math) so it can never bleed; it floats over the workbench's bottom seam as "the mind pulling up a work-surface while still voyaging." HIDE (all sanctioned by existing lab media-rule precedent): `.sb-shell--manufacture .left-console, .sb-shell--manufacture .right-console{display:none}` (drei-Html 3D-anchored consoles — lab hides them <620px/<900px; they cannot live over a centered workbench); `.sb-shell--manufacture .terminal-log{display:none}` (its `left:calc(50% - 372px)` centered geometry would sit under the editor — lab hides <1100px; its narration is reintroduced as a tiny product ticker, see workbench); `.sb-shell--manufacture .core-readout{display:none}` (SUPERMIND/0N hero belongs to the home voyage); `.sb-shell--manufacture .hud-footer{display:none}` (lab hides <1100px); `.sb-shell--manufacture .bottom-scrim{display:none}` (its 220px gradient would muddy the workbench bottom). The portal `#hud-portal-root` is NOT moved (ported fact relied on). `is-booting/is-booted` classes are NOT stripped (HUD visibility depends on them).
designs.workbenchPlacement: The editor + preview live in `.sb-workbench-stage`, an absolute layer that sits OVER the fullscreen brain (z-index:2, above `.scene-layer`, below the 46px topbar and the floating command-bar). It is inset from the page edges (`inset: 58px 24px 92px 24px` — clear of the top status line and the bottom directive bar) so the brain visibly drifts in the margin around all four sides — the panels are clearly floating IN the void, not a flat IDE filling the frame. Two translucent glass slabs side by side via the existing `.wb`/`.wb-pane` flex (already in shell.css): the editor `.wb-editor` (the darker, denser glass — `rgba(8,9,12,0.86)` + backdrop blur, so the brain bleeds faintly through its chrome and edges) takes 1.6 flex; the preview `.wb-preview` takes 1 flex. I round the panel corners more and add a soft outer glow + 1px starlit border so each slab reads as a lens suspended in space rather than a window. The panels animate in with `workspaceIn` (rise + fade, already defined) — the work-surface RISES out of the infinite when summoned and sinks back on return. Crucially: because the brain is fullscreen behind, switching modes only toggles the `.sb-shell--manufacture` class and mounts/unmounts the workbench layer — the single `<WorkspaceCanvas/>` is never touched, never remounted, boot never re-runs (constraint 3 honored).
designs.themeFit: This is the purest embodiment of "AN AUTONOMOUS AI-OS SUPERBRAIN TRAVELLING CONSTANT INTO THE DEEP-VAST KNOWLEDGEABLE INFINITE SPACE." The mind never shrinks and never stops — manufacturing happens literally INSIDE its voyaging space, not in a box bolted beside it. The brain keeps its full canon framing and perpetual drift behind translucent work-surfaces, so the operator is always aware they are working within a living superintelligence that is still moving through the knowledge-galaxy (sky=VOYAGE stars drift past the panel edges, visible through the glass). The editor is the mind pulling a lens out of the infinite to do focused work while continuing to travel; the floating command-bar is the directive line into that still-voyaging mind; the thin top status line is the ship's instrument readout (LATENCY/AUTONOMY/FIDELITY/SKY/Supervised) confirming the voyage continues. Nothing about the journey pauses to manufacture — the constant travel is preserved byte-for-byte.

designs.implementation: PORT-SAFE, product-only. NO ported file touched. (1) SuperbrainShell.jsx (editable): delete nothing structural — keep the ONE `<WorkspaceCanvas/>` in `.sb-brain-stage`; add a product veil element inside the manufacture branch: `<div className="sb-void-veil" aria-hidden />`; render `<div className="sb-workbench-stage">` over it; import a NEW `./manufacturing.css`. (2) shell.css (editable, product-only): REMOVE the `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage{ height:42vh; transform:translateZ(0) }` block entirely — in this direction the brain is NEVER docked, it stays fullscreen `inset:0`, so the camera-crop and 3D-anchor-clip traps are designed out. Re-target `.sb-workbench-stage` to `position:absolute; inset:58px 24px 92px 24px; z-index:2` and add `.sb-void-veil{position:absolute; inset:0; z-index:1; background:radial-gradient(120% 90% at 50% 42%, rgba(4,6,12,0) 38%, rgba(4,6,12,0.55) 100%); pointer-events:none}` so editor text has contrast while the brain still shows through margins/glass. Round `.wb-pane` corners to 18px, add outer glow. (3) NEW manufacturing.css (product-only, UNLAYERED, imported AFTER superbrain.css so it beats @layer components — pitfall noted): all HUD overrides above, every selector prefixed `.sb-shell--manufacture` so HOME and `?ui=superbrain`/classic stay byte-perfect (pitfall: never unscoped). The single load-bearing fix is the command-bar width: `.sb-shell--manufacture .command-bar{position:fixed; bottom:18px; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); width:min(680px, calc(100% - 360px)); margin:0}` replacing the `100vw` math (with `!important` only if the layered rule or the <900px `width:calc(100vw-28px)` media rule at superbrain.css:1561 still wins — verify in his browser). (4) NEW product TopBar is NOT required — I reuse the ported `.topbar` thinned, which keeps the live telemetry + sovereignty levers wired for free (no rebuild, no data re-binding). (5) Optional tiny activity ticker bottom-right: a small product component subscribing to `cognitionBus.subscribeCognition` (importable, not editable) to replace the hidden `.terminal-log` narration. Mode switch only toggles the `.sb-shell--manufacture` class + mounts/unmounts the workbench layer; `<WorkspaceCanvas/>` is stable (constraint 3). Parity check: load `?ui=superbrain` (home) and `?ui=classic` after — both must be visually identical to pre-change goldens in HIS browser (FIDELITY law).
designs.gpuNote: Honest: this is the MOST EXPENSIVE direction on the brain side, because the brain stays FULLSCREEN (full native pixel count, full camera drift) instead of docking to fewer pixels — it does NOT take the constraint-5 "docked = cheaper" saving that a small-box dock would. On a 16GB laptop also running local LLM inference + Monaco + a live-preview iframe, the brain is rendering at full resolution the whole time the workbench is up. Mitigations, all already exposed: (a) the QualityTierProvider/TIER_DPR system (WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:21-25) lets a deliberately LIGHTER tier be selected for manufacturing — I keep FIDELITY reachable in the thin topbar so the operator can drop DPR/effects while working, recovering most of the headroom without a redesign; (b) the workbench glass uses `backdrop-filter: blur()` over a live WebGL canvas, which is itself GPU work — I keep blur radii modest (≤14px) and the veil is a cheap static radial gradient, not a second blur pass; (c) Monaco + iframe are unavoidable. Net: heavier than a docked brain by design, but bounded by the existing tier lever and honest about it. If the laptop chokes, the fallback within this same direction is a FIDELITY tier drop, not a layout change.
designs.risks: 1) GPU is the real risk (see note): fullscreen brain + blurred glass + Monaco + iframe + local inference simultaneously may stutter; relies on the operator dropping FIDELITY tier — must verify the docked-cheaper budget isn't actually needed on his machine. 2) READABILITY: a busy/bright voyaging brain behind translucent editor glass can hurt code legibility; the `.sb-void-veil` radial scrim + the editor's `0.86` opacity backdrop mitigate, but this needs his-browser before/after screenshots (FIDELITY law) — if text contrast fails, the editor pane must go more opaque, eroding the "float in space" feel (the central tension of this direction). 3) command-bar override specificity: the <900px media rule (superbrain.css:1561) sets `width:calc(100vw-28px)` and the base rule lives in @layer components; my unlayered product rule should win, but if his viewport hits that breakpoint or a `!important` exists, the bar could still escape — must be verified live, possibly needs `!important`. 4) backdrop-filter over a WebGL canvas is occasionally flaky across GPUs/browsers (can render opaque or drop the blur) — needs verification in HIS exact browser. 5) Cannot ship if parity on home/classic regresses — every override is `.sb-shell--manufacture`-scoped to prevent that, but it must be proven, not assumed. 6) The brain visible through/around the panels means knowledge-stars drifting behind code could feel distracting to some operators during deep focus — a per-session "dim the voyage" veil-opacity control (product-only) is the safety valve, but adds scope.

designs.name: VOYAGE COCKPIT — The Brain Rides Shotgun
designs.oneLineConcept: The voyaging brain docks as a tall, full-height NAVIGATOR rail down the left edge — a companion mind riding alongside the work — while the Monaco editor + live preview float as glass slabs in the main field, the whole page reading as one cockpit cruising through the infinite.
designs.brainPresentation: The brain lives in a tall, narrow LEFT RAIL — `.sb-brain-stage` becomes `position:absolute; left:0; top:0; bottom:0; width:clamp(300px, 26vw, 380px); transform:translateZ(0)` (the transform makes this rail the containing block for the brain's `position:fixed; inset:0` `.superbrain-experience`, so the whole fixed experience + `#hud-portal-root` resolve INTO the rail, never the window). This is a TALL box (full viewport height), which is exactly what the fixed-FOV `CameraDrift` (z≈7.5 hero framing, fixed vertical FOV) wants — a tall-and-narrow aspect lets the camera frame the 3.02-scale brain WITHOUT the top/bottom crop that a 42vh thin strip caused. The brain is RE-SIZED (technique 1: native-res render at fewer pixels via r3f's ResizeObserver), NOT scaled — so the HUD text stays crisp and legible, and the GPU paints a narrow column instead of the full screen. It keeps voyaging the entire time: same scene, same shaders, same ±0.12 dolly wave, same 60s orbit, same parallax — the canvas never remounts (only the stage box resizes on the mode-class toggle), so BootSequence never re-runs. The brain reads as a living porthole down the side of the cockpit: the mind is literally travelling beside you while you work.
designs.hudHandling: All overrides are scoped under `.sb-shell--manufacture` in a NEW unlayered product stylesheet (`manufacturing.css`) loaded AFTER superbrain.css, so they beat the @layer-components ported rules and NEVER touch home/classic. KEEP-IN-RAIL (these are portal-rendered, absolute-to-`.superbrain-experience`, so they already follow the rail box): `.command-bar` — KEEP as the page's directive line but RE-ANCHOR + UN-ESCAPE the 100vw width: `.sb-shell--manufacture .command-bar{ position:fixed; left:auto; right:18px; bottom:18px; width:min(560px, calc(100vw - 420px)); transform:none; }` — this floats it across the WORKBENCH bottom (the rail is only ~26vw, too narrow for an 880px bar), and the explicit width kills the 100vw escape (constraint-4 trap; the lab's own <900px rule rewrites this width, proving it's overridable). HIDE-IN-RAIL: `.sb-shell--manufacture .right-console{display:none}` and `.sb-shell--manufacture .console-surge--reverse{display:none}` (drei `<Html position={[4.8,…]}>` 3D-anchored — would project off-screen-right of a narrow rail and cram; lab precedent hides it <900px, superbrain.css:1526). `.sb-shell--manufacture .left-console, .console-surge{display:none}` (drei `<Html position={[-4.8,…]}>`; the objective-card is redundant with the workbench, lab hides <620px). `.sb-shell--manufacture .core-readout{display:none}` (the SUPERMIND/0N hero belongs to the home voyage). `.sb-shell--manufacture .bottom-scrim{display:none}` (the 220px gradient would darken the rail's lower edge oddly). `.sb-shell--manufacture .hud-footer{display:none}` (lab hides <1100px). `.sb-shell--manufacture .terminal-log{display:none}` in the rail — its `left:calc(50% - 372px)` geometry assumes an 880px centered bar and collides in a narrow column (lab hides <1100px). REPOSITION/THIN the topbar: I LIFT the sovereignty row out of the rail entirely — `.sb-shell--manufacture .topbar{display:none}` — and reproduce the sacred levers (FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND, the Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER `.secure-button` posture, the live LATENCY/AUTONOMY status-dot, and a small brand mark) in a product-built slim app top-bar (`.sb-topbar`) that spans the FULL page above both rail and workbench. The product top-bar is bound to the same live data via the importable (un-edited) ported lib — `metricsStore.useMetric`, `aiosAdapter.getLastTelemetry`, `cognitionBus.subscribeCognition` — so the readouts are real, not faked.
designs.workbenchPlacement: The workbench occupies the main field to the RIGHT of the brain rail: `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-workbench-stage{ position:absolute; left:clamp(300px,26vw,380px); right:0; top:48px; bottom:0; padding:14px; }` (top:48px clears the product `.sb-topbar`). Inside, the existing `.wb` flex layout is reused unchanged: `.wb-editor` (Monaco/CodeCanvas, flex 1.6) and `.wb-preview` (sandboxed LivePreview, flex 1) sit as the existing glass slabs (`.wb-pane`: rounded, translucent, `backdrop-filter`, deep `box-shadow`). They float IN the infinite space because the page background is the SAME `#010307` voyage void and the slabs are translucent glass with no opaque page chrome behind them — the brain's nebula glow bleeds along the left rail seam, so the editor reads as a work-surface the mind pulled up beside itself, lit by the same starfield. A thin un-bordered seam (no hard divider) between rail and workbench keeps it as one continuous cockpit rather than two boxed panes. The floated `.command-bar` sits at the workbench's bottom-right as the always-present directive line, so issuing a command and watching the editor change feel like one act inside the moving cockpit.
designs.themeFit: This is the literal cockpit of "an autonomous AI-OS superbrain travelling constant into the deep-vast knowledgeable infinite space." The brain never stops — it voyages full-height down the left the entire session (same VOYAGE sky, same MemoryGalaxy knowledge-stars, same perpetual drift), so the operator works WHILE the mind keeps moving through the infinite, exactly the brief's "the editor is the mind pulling up a work-surface WHILE still moving." A side companion reads as a navigator riding shotgun: the mind cruises the knowledge-space on your left while the manufacturing surface floats in the same void on your right — one shared infinite, not an IDE bolted on. The product top-bar carries the sovereignty/Supervised posture across the whole cockpit, keeping the AI-OS trust story (supervised, audited) present at the helm. The tall porthole is the most honest expression of "never frozen": you literally watch the brain travel beside your work.

designs.implementation: PORT-SAFE, product-only. NO ported file is touched. (1) NEW FILE `frontend/src/superbrain/manufacturing.css` — all `.sb-shell--manufacture`-scoped overrides above (unlayered, imported in SuperbrainShell.jsx AFTER superbrain.css so it wins over @layer components; use `!important` only where the lab uses it, e.g. console `animation:none`). (2) EDIT `shell.css` (product-owned): replace the 42vh top-band block with the LEFT-RAIL geometry — `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage{ inset:auto; left:0; top:0; bottom:0; width:clamp(300px,26vw,380px); transform:translateZ(0); overflow:hidden; }` and `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-workbench-stage{ left:clamp(300px,26vw,380px); right:0; top:48px; bottom:0; }`. Keep `.sb-mode-toggle` (bottom-left pill). (3) NEW FILE `frontend/src/superbrain/ProductTopBar.jsx` — the slim full-width `.sb-topbar`: brand mark + live status (subscribe to `metricsStore.useMetric` for LATENCY/AUTONOMY, `aiosAdapter.getLastTelemetry` for chainValid→Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER) + the FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND sovereignty buttons (reusing the same state the ported HUD reads, so they stay the real operator levers). Imports only the un-edited ported lib (allowed). (4) EDIT `SuperbrainShell.jsx` — add `import './manufacturing.css'` and `import ProductTopBar from './ProductTopBar'`; render `{manufacturing && <ProductTopBar/>}` above the stages. The SINGLE `<WorkspaceCanvas/>` stays exactly where it is (one instance, persistent; only the stage class toggles — never conditionally rendered, so no boot re-run). DOCK TECHNIQUE: technique-1 (transform-as-containing-block + sized box) on `.sb-brain-stage`, tall-narrow aspect so the fixed-FOV camera frames the brain; `#hud-portal-root` stays inside `.superbrain-experience` (untouched). Workbench (`Workbench.jsx`, `CodeCanvas`, `LivePreview`) reused as-is.
designs.gpuNote: Cheaper than fullscreen, honestly. The brain paints a tall column of clamp(300-380px) × full-height ≈ 26% of screen width, so roughly a QUARTER of the fullscreen pixel count (technique-1 = native-res render of a smaller drawing buffer, not a downscaled fullscreen render, so the saving is real — unlike technique-2 scale(), which renders full pixels then shrinks). That headroom matters because the same 16GB laptop is simultaneously running local Ollama inference + Monaco's worker + a live-preview iframe. Caveat: a tall rail still runs the full SuperbrainScene (NeuralAura, NervousSystem, MemoryGalaxy, PostFX) every frame — it's fewer pixels, not fewer objects — so per-frame scene cost (geometry/shaders) is unchanged; only fragment/fill cost drops. If a tight run still stutters, the sovereignty SKY/FIDELITY levers stay reachable in the product top-bar and the QualityTierProvider/TIER_DPR path lets the operator drop the docked brain to a deliberately lighter tier. Net: clearly better than the fullscreen home form, with a manual escape hatch.
designs.risks: 1) CAMERA FRAMING is the real unknown: CameraDrift hard-codes z≈7.5 and lookAt(brainDriftX*0.35, 0.62, -1.2) for a WIDE viewport. A tall-narrow rail (aspect ~0.4) with fixed VERTICAL FOV will frame the brain's height fine but may crop its left/right edges, and the off-center lookAt could push the brain toward one side of the rail — needs verification in his browser; the only product-side mitigation (camera is ported/un-editable) is tuning rail WIDTH (the clamp) or, worst case, a mild technique-2 scale() inside the rail to recover framing at the cost of GPU saving. This must be parity-checked with before/after screenshots (FIDELITY-IS-SACRED). 2) The floated `.command-bar` `width:min(560px, calc(100vw-420px))` is tuned to the rail width + workbench — at very narrow windows the 100vw term could still collide with the rail; needs a small-window media check. 3) Rebuilding the sovereignty controls in `ProductTopBar.jsx` risks drift from the ported `.secure-button`/fidelity state — it must bind to the SAME store (metricsStore/aiosAdapter), not a copy, or the Supervised/FIDELITY posture could disagree with the brain's own HUD (a trust-story bug). 4) `manufacturing.css` MUST stay scoped under `.sb-shell--manufacture` and unlayered; any unscoped `.topbar`/`.command-bar`/`.left-console` rule would corrupt the byte-perfect HOME voyage and the default ?ui=superbrain mount. 5) Hiding both side consoles drops live intake/agent-mesh telemetry from the work form; if the operator wants it back, it's a later product AgentStrip (bound to cognitionBus), not in this pass. 6) A full-height brain beside a bright editor is high visual contrast — the seam glow must be tuned so the nebula doesn't wash the Monaco text; verify legibility.

designs.name: VOYAGER ORB — the co-pilot mind in the corner of the work
designs.oneLineConcept: The full voyaging brain shrinks to a small, perpetually-travelling orb pinned in the lower-right corner with a single-line cognition readout; the Monaco editor + live preview become the main stage, two glass slabs floating in the same starfield the orb keeps voyaging through — the mind as an ever-present co-pilot watching over the work, cheapest possible on GPU.
designs.brainPresentation: A single persistent WorkspaceCanvas, never re-mounted — only its stage box is resized by toggling the .sb-shell--manufacture class on a stable ancestor. The orb is a ~300x300px (clamped 240-340px responsive) rounded corner dock pinned bottom-right (right:18px; bottom:18px), masked to a soft circle via border-radius + a radial mask-image so the brain reads as a contained luminous PRESENCE, not a cropped rectangle. It is achieved with dock technique 1 (transform-as-containing-block + sized box): .sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage gets `transform: translateZ(0)` (already present) plus an explicit small square-ish box. Square-ish aspect (≈1:1) is the deliberate fix for the fixed-FOV CameraDrift crop — a thin 42vh strip cropped the 3.02-scale brain top/bottom and projected the world-x=±4.8 consoles off-band; a near-square box lets the existing z≈7.5 hero framing still center the brain. It keeps VOYAGING untouched: r3f's ResizeObserver re-fits the Canvas to the smaller box with NO remount, so CameraDrift's ±0.12 dolly wave, 60s orbit, pointer parallax and the MemoryGalaxy starfield all keep running — the orb is a live travelling mind, just small. No transform:scale (that would render full fullscreen pixels then downscale = no GPU win and tiny HUD); the sized-box approach renders the real brain at far fewer pixels. Above/over the orb sits ONE product-built single-line readout (a new .sb-orb-readout div in SuperbrainShell, NOT a ported element) showing the live cognition feed line + a pulsing status dot — bound to the unmodified ported lib (cognitionBus.subscribeCognition / metricsStore.useMetric) so it reads the same heartbeat the home HUD does, proving the orb is thinking, not frozen.
designs.hudHandling: Everything ported that fights a tiny corner orb is HIDDEN via product-only overrides scoped under .sb-shell--manufacture (a new manufacturing.css imported AFTER superbrain.css, unlayered so it beats superbrain.css's @layer components rules). HIDE (all sanctioned by existing lab media-query precedents): the two 3D-anchored drei-Html consoles `.sb-shell--manufacture .left-console{display:none}` and `.sb-shell--manufacture .right-console{display:none}` (lab hides at <620px/<900px — they cannot live in a small orb box and their drei wrapper transform is not CSS-targetable); the centered hero `.sb-shell--manufacture .core-readout{display:none}` and `.sb-shell--manufacture .hud-footer{display:none}` (lab hides hud-footer <1100px); the gradient `.sb-shell--manufacture .bottom-scrim{display:none}` (its 220px gradient would darken the orb's lower arc oddly); and the ported `.sb-shell--manufacture .topbar{display:none}` entirely — the whole sovereignty row moves to product chrome. The ported `.command-bar` is also HIDDEN in the orb (`.sb-shell--manufacture .command-bar{display:none}`) because its width uses 100vw and it is the soul of the directive line — it is REBUILT as product UI in the page top bar (see implementation) so it stops escaping the box and becomes the page-global directive line. KEEP-AS-PRODUCT (rebuilt, bound to the same telemetry, NOT the ported nodes): the sovereignty levers FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND and the Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER shield (ported classes .secure-button / .system-summary / .fidelity-button) are reproduced in a new product TopBar.jsx reading the same state (aiosAdapter.getLastTelemetry → telemetry.chainValid/approvalHold, metricsStore latency/autonomy) — these are sacred operator controls and stay reachable, just not crammed in the orb. The live cognition feed (ported .terminal-log) is HIDDEN on the orb (`.sb-shell--manufacture .terminal-log{display:none}`, lab hides <1100px) and its single most-recent line is surfaced in the product .sb-orb-readout beside the orb. Brand: the GAGOS mark goes into the product TopBar (small mark, build-tag dropped). Result: zero ported HUD nodes live inside the orb box — only the pure brain scene + starfield render there, so nothing can overlap.
designs.workbenchPlacement: The editor + preview are the MAIN STAGE and own the full viewport between a slim product top bar and the page bottom. The existing .sb-workbench-stage is re-laid: instead of top:42vh, it becomes top:48px (under the product top bar), inset left/right/bottom to small gutters, z-index:2. Inside it the existing .wb flex row (.wb-editor 1.6fr Monaco / .wb-preview 1fr LivePreview) stays as-is. The slabs read as FLOATING IN THE INFINITE because: (1) the .sb-brain-stage orb is the only opaque thing — the rest of .sb-shell behind the slabs is the live brain experience's starfield bleeding through the workbench's gutter padding and the translucent slab edges; (2) the slabs keep their glass treatment (.wb-editor already backdrop-filter:blur+saturate, semi-transparent rgba(8,9,12,0.86)) so the voyaging starfield is faintly visible THROUGH the editor chrome and in the 14px gutters around every slab — the void is always at the margins; (3) the orb sits in the lower-right gutter OVERLAPPING nothing, so the eye reads "two work-surfaces the mind pulled up, the mind itself still travelling in the corner." The slabs animate in with the existing workspaceIn keyframe (a settle from the void), reinforcing "summoned from the infinite," never a flat IDE.
designs.themeFit: The core theme is an autonomous superbrain travelling CONSTANT into the deep-vast infinite — and this direction is the most literal embodiment of "the brain must NEVER stop travelling, even in the manufacturing form." The orb is the same canon brain, same MemoryGalaxy starfield, same CameraDrift voyage motion — byte-identical scene, just framed small. It never freezes: while you code, the orb in the corner keeps dollying, orbiting and parallaxing through the knowledge-stars, with its single-line readout ticking the live cognition feed — a co-pilot mind that is demonstrably STILL VOYAGING while it pulls up a work-surface. The workbench slabs floating over the same starfield (visible in the gutters and through the glass) make the editor read as "the mind pulling up a work-surface WHILE still moving through the infinite," exactly the brief — not an IDE bolted on, but tools summoned into the voyage. The persistent ever-present orb is the AI-OS co-pilot made visible: small, constant, autonomous, always travelling onward into the vast.
designs.implementation: PORT-SAFE, product-only — no ported file touched. (A) SuperbrainShell.jsx (editable): keep the SINGLE <WorkspaceCanvas/> inside .sb-brain-stage exactly as now (never conditionally unmount it — only the wrapper class toggles, satisfying constraint 3). Add three product nodes rendered only when manufacturing: a product <TopBar/> (new file), the product .sb-orb-readout single-line div (positioned over the orb, fed by cognitionBus.subscribeCognition), and keep <Workbench/> in .sb-workbench-stage. Keep the existing .sb-mode-toggle pill (move it to left:18px just above nothing). (B) New file frontend/src/superbrain/TopBar.jsx (product-only): a 48px flex bar with the GAGOS mark (small), a rebuilt directive input + Execute button (the page-global SUPERMIND command line, replacing the hidden ported .command-bar, wired to the same turn/approval action), and a right cluster reproducing FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND + the Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER shield bound to aiosAdapter.getLastTelemetry + metricsStore.useMetric (importable, not edited). (C) New file frontend/src/superbrain/manufacturing.css (product-only, imported in SuperbrainShell AFTER superbrain.css, NOT inside any @layer so it beats the layered ported rules; use !important only where a ported rule uses it). Contents — all scoped under .sb-shell--manufacture so HOME/classic stay byte-perfect: hide the topbar, command-bar, terminal-log, left-console, right-console, core-readout, hud-footer, bottom-scrim (per hudHandling); style the orb dock + readout + the new top bar. (D) shell.css (editable) changes: replace the 42vh top-band rules — .sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage becomes inset:auto; right:18px; bottom:18px; width:clamp(240px,24vw,340px); height:clamp(240px,24vw,340px); transform:translateZ(0); border-radius:24px; overflow:hidden; mask-image:radial-gradient(circle at 50% 50%, #000 62%, transparent 78%); pointer-events:none on the box (so editor clicks pass, the orb is ambient) — the transform still makes it the containing block so the fixed brain experience resolves INTO this small box. .sb-workbench-stage becomes top:48px; left/right/bottom gutters; the orb's bottom-right corner sits in the workbench's lower-right gutter overlapping nothing. (E) DOCK TECHNIQUE: technique 1 (transform-containing-block + sized near-square box). Because every ported portal-HUD node that would have followed the box into the orb is display:none'd, and the two consoles are drei-anchored + hidden, NOTHING ported renders inside the orb — only the brain scene, so the 100vw command-bar escape and the console-clip are both eliminated by hiding rather than by fighting CSS. No second canvas, no remount, boot runs once.
designs.gpuNote: Cheapest of all docks — this is the GPU win. The orb renders the brain at ≈300x300 device-independent px (DPR-scaled by the existing QualityTierProvider/TIER_DPR), i.e. on the order of ~5-7% of a 1920x1080 fullscreen pixel count, so the fragment/shader load drops roughly an order of magnitude vs the home voyage. Honest caveats on a 16GB laptop also running local LLM inference + Monaco + a preview iframe: (1) the brain's per-frame JS (CameraDrift easing, orbit, any particle updates) still runs at full rate regardless of pixel count — geometry/animation cost is unchanged, only rasterization shrinks; so CPU-side scene work is NOT reduced, only GPU fill. (2) Monaco + the live-preview iframe each take their own compositor layers and the orb's backdrop mask adds one more composited layer — fine on this GPU but real. (3) Mitigation already available: the orb is the natural place to let the operator drop FIDELITY to a lighter tier (the sovereignty lever stays reachable in the product TopBar), making it deliberately lighter during heavy inference. Net: clearly cheaper than fullscreen and the cheapest of the three candidate dock shapes, but be honest that the savings are GPU-fill, not total — the scene's CPU animation cost persists (that is the price of keeping it genuinely voyaging, which is the point).
designs.risks: 1) CameraDrift framing in a near-square box is unverified: z≈7.5 + BRAIN_SCALE 3.02 were tuned for a wide hero viewport; a ~1:1 box may frame the brain slightly large or off-center, and the world-x=±4.8 console anchors (now hidden) confirm the scene expects width. MUST be proven in HIS browser with before/after screenshots; if the brain reads too zoomed, the only port-safe lever is box aspect/size (cannot edit the camera) — fallback is a slightly wider 4:3 orb. 2) The radial mask-image softening the orb edge is cosmetic; if it clips the brain's outer glow oddly at small size it should be loosened or dropped (pure border-radius). 3) Rebuilding the command bar + sovereignty cluster as product UI means re-implementing their wiring against the ported lib (cognitionBus/metricsStore/aiosAdapter) — if those exports' signatures differ from assumption, the product TopBar needs to match them exactly; it must read the SAME telemetry (chainValid/approvalHold) so Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER never diverges from the real audit posture. 4) Theme risk: a corner orb is the most "co-pilot," least "hero" reading of the brain — some may feel the voyaging mind is demoted to a HUD widget rather than the lead; if the operator wants the brain to still feel like the protagonist, this direction trades presence for workbench dominance (a deliberate, honest tradeoff of this assigned direction). 5) pointer-events:none on the orb box means the orb is non-interactive (ambient only) — acceptable since all controls live in the product TopBar, but the "Return to voyage" path must stay obvious (keep the .sb-mode-toggle pill). 6) Don't strip .is-booting/.is-booted on .superbrain-experience when wrapping, or the HUD/scene visibility gating breaks; verify the orb still completes its one-shot boot.

designs.name: CINEMATIC SPLIT-HORIZON — The Voyage Above, The Forge Below
designs.oneLineConcept: The brain keeps voyaging across a clean, HUD-stripped horizon band at the top of the page; every heavy cockpit panel is product-hidden, and only two essentials are relocated into the shell's own chrome — a slim status ribbon riding the horizon's bottom edge, and one unified command line floating across the page bottom — so the editor and preview read as work-surfaces the still-moving mind pulled up beneath itself, never a flat IDE bolted on.
designs.brainPresentation: The brain stays the canon WorkspaceCanvas scene, byte-untouched, voyaging continuously (CameraDrift's perpetual dolly wave + 60s orbit + pointer parallax all keep running — the box only resizes, the scene never remounts, so boot never re-runs and the mind never freezes). It is docked into a horizon BAND across the top of the manufacturing page using the existing transform-as-containing-block technique already in shell.css:33-42 (`.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage { transform: translateZ(0) }`), so the fixed `.superbrain-experience` (superbrain.css:105-111) resolves into this box and r3f re-fits the Canvas via its ResizeObserver at native res / fewer pixels. CRITICAL FIX vs the current 42vh strip: I raise the band to a camera-friendly aspect — height: clamp(340px, 52vh, 560px) — because the fixed-FOV CameraDrift (z≈7.5, SuperbrainScene.tsx ~967) crops the 3.02-scale brain top/bottom in a thin wide strip. ~52vh gives a wide-but-not-squashed letterbox where the brain core sits whole and centered, reading as a true horizon rather than a sliver. No transform:scale() — the brain renders crisp at native res, not a downscaled thumbnail. Motion is preserved exactly; it is a resize, never a freeze or a screenshot.
designs.hudHandling:
Every override is scoped under `.sb-shell--manufacture` so HOME and ?ui=superbrain stay byte-perfect, and lives in an UNLAYERED product stylesheet loaded after superbrain.css (unlayered author CSS beats @layer components, superbrain.css:104 — so no specificity war).

HIDE (the heavy cockpit — these are the clutter):
- Command bar: `.sb-shell--manufacture .command-bar { display: none }` (superbrain.css:1212; its width uses 100vw at :1217 — hiding it kills the width-escape trap entirely; I re-provide its function as a product command line below).
- Right caste panel: `.sb-shell--manufacture .right-console { display: none }` (superbrain.css:421/440; drei-Html-anchored at world x=+4.8, SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1097 — cannot be CSS-repositioned, and the lab itself hides it <900px at :1526, so this drop is sanctioned).
- Left console: `.sb-shell--manufacture .left-console { display: none }` (superbrain.css:421/440; anchored x=-4.8, SuperbrainHUD.tsx:1046; lab hides <620px at :1573).
- Terminal log: `.sb-shell--manufacture .terminal-log { display: none }` (superbrain.css:1159; lab hides <1100px at :1509).
- Topbar (whole ported header): `.sb-shell--manufacture .topbar { display: none }` (superbrain.css:238) — I rebuild only the sovereignty levers I keep, in product chrome.
- Decoration: `.sb-shell--manufacture .core-readout, .sb-shell--manufacture .hud-footer, .sb-shell--manufacture .bottom-scrim { display: none }` (superbrain.css:383 / 1370 / 226 — the 220px scrim would darken the band's lower edge oddly; the SUPERMIND/0N hero belongs to the home voyage).

KEEP / RELOCATE (the essentials, rebuilt as product UI bound to live data — never crammed into the band):
- Sovereignty levers FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND + the Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER shield (ported `.secure-button`, superbrain.css:355) → rebuilt in a product `<ShellTopBar>` (new file) spanning the full page above the band, reading live `aiosAdapter.getLastTelemetry()` (chainValid/approvalHold) and the same sky/surface state already owned product-side in WorkspaceCanvas. The GAGOS mark (`.brand-mark`) is reproduced small; the AUTONOMOUS CORE build-tag is dropped (lab drops `.build-tag` <620px, superbrain.css:1573).
- CORE ONLINE / LATENCY / AUTONOMY readouts → a slim product STATUS RIBBON pinned to the horizon band's bottom edge, reading the same `metricsStore.useMetric` channels (importable, not edited).
- The OBSERVE/SYNTHESIZE/ORCHESTRATE mode rail (the one real control inside the hidden left console) → small product segmented control in the ShellTopBar.

So: nothing operator-meaningful is lost; the band becomes a clean voyaging horizon, and the controls move to chrome where they have room to breathe.
designs.workbenchPlacement: The existing Workbench (editor `.wb-editor` + preview `.wb-preview`, glass slabs already in shell.css:90-175) sits in `.sb-workbench-stage` filling the page below the horizon band (top: clamp(340px,52vh,560px) to match the new band height). It reads as floating IN the infinite space, not a flat IDE, via four port-safe moves: (1) the stage stays transparent — the voyaging cosmos is the page background, the slabs hover over it; (2) the band-to-workbench seam is a soft gradient scrim (product CSS, ~80px) so the brain's nebula bleeds down behind the slabs' tops instead of a hard IDE divider; (3) the glass slabs keep their existing backdrop-filter so the moving starfield faintly refracts through their edges; (4) the slabs get a slightly larger top gap and a faint downward shadow so they read as suspended beneath the horizon, like surfaces the mind drew up under itself. The unified command line floats over the workbench's bottom, just clear of the slabs, completing the read of "the mind pulling up a work-surface while still moving."
designs.themeFit: The whole composition IS the theme made literal: an autonomous superbrain travelling constant into the deep-vast knowledgeable infinite space. The top band is the VOYAGE — the brain never stops moving through its MemoryGalaxy of knowledge-as-stars, fully visible as a clean horizon (not crammed, not frozen). The workbench below is the mind reaching DOWN into that same infinite to pull up a work-surface while still travelling — the editor and preview float in the cosmos, the starfield refracts through their glass, and the seamless scrim means there is no "page break" between voyage and forge: it is one continuous deep space, brain above, the work it manifests below. The relocated command line is the mind's directive channel kept always-present, so even mid-manufacture the operator is speaking to a voyaging intelligence, not a docked tool. Hiding the cockpit clutter is precisely what lets the INFINITE read as infinite — a clean horizon is vaster than a busy one.

designs.implementation:
PORT-SAFE, product-only. No ported file touched.

NEW product files:
1. `frontend/src/workbench/manufacturing.css` — the override sheet, imported in SuperbrainShell.jsx AFTER `./superbrain.css`, UNLAYERED (beats @layer components). All rules scoped `.sb-shell--manufacture …`. Contents: the HIDE block (command-bar/right-console/left-console/terminal-log/topbar/core-readout/hud-footer/bottom-scrim → display:none, per classes above); the seam scrim; and a width-safety belt for the rare case any kept ported element re-appears.
2. `frontend/src/workbench/ShellTopBar.jsx` — product header: small GAGOS mark, OBSERVE/SYNTHESIZE/ORCHESTRATE segmented control, FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND buttons, Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER pill. Binds to importable (un-edited) lib: `aiosAdapter.getLastTelemetry()`, `metricsStore.useMetric`, `cognitionBus.subscribeCognition`. Mode + sky/surface lift to SuperbrainShell state (the toggles WorkspaceCanvas already owns are passed down, or mirrored via the same localStorage keys SKY_STORAGE_KEY/SURFACE_STORAGE_KEY).
3. `frontend/src/workbench/StatusRibbon.jsx` — CORE ONLINE / LATENCY / AUTONOMY, same metric channels; pinned to band bottom edge.
4. `frontend/src/workbench/CommandLine.jsx` — the unified directive input; on submit calls the SAME `sendDirective` path WorkspaceCanvas uses (passed down as a prop from a shared handler, so there is ONE directive pipeline). Floats over the workbench bottom.

EDIT (product-only, allowed):
- `SuperbrainShell.jsx`: import manufacturing.css; render `<ShellTopBar/>`, `<StatusRibbon/>`, `<CommandLine/>` only when `manufacturing`; keep the SINGLE `<WorkspaceCanvas/>` exactly as-is (constraint 3) — only the `.sb-shell--manufacture` class toggles, never a conditional canvas mount/unmount.
- `shell.css`: raise `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage` height 42vh → `clamp(340px,52vh,560px)`; set `.sb-workbench-stage { top: clamp(340px,52vh,560px) }`; add the floating command-line + ribbon layout boxes. Keep the transform technique untouched.

LAYOUT TECHNIQUE: unchanged transform-as-containing-block (shell.css:33-42) — the only winning dock per constraint 4. The product chrome (ShellTopBar/ribbon/command-line) lives OUTSIDE `.sb-brain-stage` so it is plain shell DOM positioned against the page, never inside the brain's fixed experience — no 100vw escape possible. `#hud-portal-root` is left exactly where it is (inside .superbrain-experience) — not moved.
designs.gpuNote: Honestly cheaper than fullscreen home, and the cheapest of the three dock techniques. The brain renders at native res into a box of clamp(340px,52vh,560px) height × page width — on a 1080p laptop that is roughly 45-55% of the fullscreen pixel count, and fill-rate (the dominant cost here: PostFX composer, MSAA, the nebula) scales with pixels, so the docked brain draws materially fewer fragments than fullscreen. No transform:scale() means we do NOT pay the full-screen pixel count then downscale (technique 2's hidden cost) — we genuinely render fewer pixels. Hiding the two drei-Html consoles and the terminal-log also removes their continuous DOM/animation work. Still honest about the real budget on a 16GB laptop running Ollama + Monaco + a preview iframe: WebGL post-processing on a continuously-animating scene is never free, and the band is large (52vh) for camera-framing reasons, so this is "cheaper than fullscreen," not "cheap." Mitigations available without touching canon: the QualityTierProvider/TIER_DPR tier system is live, so a deliberately lighter tier is selectable in manufacturing, and the FIDELITY lever in the new ShellTopBar lets the operator drop fill cost on demand. GPU context-loss (Ollama evicting the GPU) is already handled by WorkspaceCanvas's restore/remount path — unchanged.
designs.risks:
1. CAMERA CROP is the make-or-break: 52vh is my judgment for keeping the fixed-FOV z≈7.5 framing whole, but I cannot edit CameraDrift, so the exact band height MUST be tuned by eye in HIS browser (the clamp may need to land at 48-58vh). If even the tallest comfortable band still crops the brain badly, the honest fallback is a SIDE dock (tall left rail) rather than a top band — a thin top strip is non-viable and I will not ship one.
2. Vertical budget: a 52vh band leaves ~48vh minus the command line for the workbench — tight on a short laptop screen. May need the band to shrink (not vanish) as viewport height drops, via a height media query in product CSS; must verify Monaco stays usable.
3. The relocated command line and mode rail must drive the SAME `sendDirective`/mode state as the (now hidden) ported ones, or the operator gets two divergent directive paths — this is wiring discipline, not styling, and is the main build risk. Until wired, the product command line is cosmetic.
4. FIDELITY-SACRED parity: this changes COMPOSITION only, never the brain scene, but it still requires before/after screenshots in HIS browser and confirmation that HOME / ?ui=superbrain render byte-identical (the `.sb-shell--manufacture` scoping is what protects them — any unscoped slip would corrupt the home voyage, the one unacceptable failure).
5. Live-data hooks (getLastTelemetry/useMetric/subscribeCognition) are assumed importable-not-editable; if any isn't cleanly importable product-side, the ribbon/shield degrade to static until a thin product adapter is added — never by editing the ported lib.

designs.name: SUMMONED FOCUS — the work-surface rises over the voyage
designs.oneLineConcept: The brain never docks: it keeps voyaging fullscreen and untouched while the workbench is summoned UP over it as a focused glass layer — a parallaxed, dimmed infinite behind a floating editor+preview, so manufacturing reads as the mind pulling a surface toward itself mid-flight, never a flat IDE bolted on.
designs.brainPresentation: Fullscreen and unchanged. This is the key move: in manufacturing mode I do NOT put a `transform` on `.sb-brain-stage` and I do NOT size it — `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage` stays `inset:0` exactly like home, so `.superbrain-experience` (position:fixed; inset:0) still resolves to the full viewport. The Canvas renders at the SAME canon framing — no aspect change, so the fixed-FOV CameraDrift (z≈7.5 hero framing, ±0.12 dolly, 60s orbit, pointer parallax, SuperbrainScene.tsx) frames the 3.02-scale brain exactly as designed. There is zero camera crop and zero drei-Html console clipping because the box never shrinks. The brain keeps voyaging — same single persistent WorkspaceCanvas, never remounted, boot never re-run (constraint 3 satisfied trivially because the stage box literally does not change). The ONLY change to the brain layer is a product-only veil: a glass scrim (`.sb-veil`) painted by the shell BETWEEN the brain and the workbench (z-index above the Canvas, below the workbench) that dims the voyage to ~38% luminance and adds a soft blur(3px) + a faint parallax drift, so the brain reads as "still travelling, now in the background of focus." Motion is preserved — I dim, I never freeze. The veil animates in over 0.4s when focus is summoned and out when dismissed, so the toggle reads as pulling focus between voyage and work rather than a hard split.
designs.hudHandling: Because the brain stays fullscreen, the ported HUD is NOT cramped — every element resolves to the full viewport exactly as on home, so nothing overlaps. The manufacturing job is therefore RECESSION, not relocation: push the voyage HUD behind the summoned focus layer and keep only the sovereignty levers reachable. All overrides are product-only, scoped under `.sb-shell--manufacture`, in a new unlayered stylesheet loaded after superbrain.css (beats @layer components per the cascade pitfall). KEEP + DIM behind the veil (they belong to the voyage and stay visible through the focus glass at the page margins): the brain-anchored side consoles `.left-console` and `.right-console` (the drei-Html caste/mode panels) — NOT hidden, because at fullscreen they project to their correct world-points with no clipping; I only soften them with `.sb-shell--manufacture .left-console, .sb-shell--manufacture .right-console{ opacity:.5; filter:saturate(.8) }` so they recede with the voyage. HIDE (decoration that fights a focus layer): `.sb-shell--manufacture .core-readout{display:none}` (the SUPERMIND/0N hero belongs to the full voyage), `.sb-shell--manufacture .hud-footer{display:none}`, `.sb-shell--manufacture .bottom-scrim{display:none}` (its 220px gradient would muddy the bottom command bar). KEEP + RAISE ABOVE the veil (the operator's live controls must stay on top of the focus layer, not buried): the topbar sovereignty row `.sb-shell--manufacture .topbar{ z-index:55 }` keeping `.brand-lockup`, `.system-summary` (CORE ONLINE/LATENCY/AUTONOMY/FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND with `.status-dot`, `.fidelity-button`) and the `.secure-button` Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER shield all live and clickable — they sit cleanly across the top because the topbar is absolute-to-the-full-viewport (64px, superbrain.css:238), which is exactly its home geometry, so it is never crammed. The `.build-tag` may shrink/drop only if the workbench title needs the room: `.sb-shell--manufacture .build-tag{display:none}` is sanctioned (lab hides it <620px). The SUPERMIND command bar `.command-bar` is KEPT and RAISED as the page's always-present directive line, but I MUST kill its 100vw width-escape (superbrain.css:1217): `.sb-shell--manufacture .command-bar{ z-index:56; width:min(680px, calc(100% - 40px)) }` — at fullscreen 100% equals the viewport so it is already correct, but I pin it to a token so it never bleeds and sits centered above the workbench. The `.terminal-log` (centered cognition feed, left:calc(50% - 372px), superbrain.css:1159) would collide WITH the workbench glass, so I re-place it as a quiet right-rail ticker: `.sb-shell--manufacture .terminal-log{ left:auto; right:20px; bottom:88px; z-index:54; opacity:.7 }` — the mind narrating its work beside the surface (pointer-events:none already). Net: zero overlap, brain canon-perfect, every sovereignty lever (FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND/Supervised + the directive bar) on top of focus, ambient HUD dimmed into the voyage behind.
designs.workbenchPlacement: The workbench is a focused glass slab summoned UP over the voyage, inset from the edges so the dimmed brain shows around it (the surface floats IN the infinite, not edge-to-edge IDE). `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-workbench-stage` is repositioned from the current top:42vh split to a centered floating panel: `position:fixed; inset: 76px 20px 96px 20px` (leaving 76px clearance for the topbar at top, 96px at the bottom for the command bar + terminal ticker), `z-index:50`, with a `workspaceRiseIn` transform (translateY(18px)+scale(.985)→0, opacity, 0.42s cubic-bezier(.16,1,.3,1)) so it RISES into focus rather than appearing. The slab gets a strong glass treatment that reads as a held surface in space: `border-radius:18px; border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.10); background:rgba(8,10,16,.62); backdrop-filter:blur(18px) saturate(1.4); box-shadow:0 50px 120px -40px rgba(0,0,0,.85), 0 0 0 1px rgba(99,102,241,.10), inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,.06)` — the indigo hairline ties it to the brain's accent. Inside, the existing `.wb` editor (Monaco/CodeCanvas) + `.wb-preview` (LivePreview iframe) keep their current 1.6/1 flex split; I only soften `.wb-editor` to a translucent `rgba(8,9,12,.74)` so a hint of the dimmed voyage bleeds through the editor gutter, reinforcing "floating in the infinite." The preview stays opaque (#f8f9fb) because a real rendered site must be true-color. The 20px margins all around mean the parallaxed brain is always visible framing the work — the operator never loses the sense of the mind still travelling around the surface they pulled up.
designs.themeFit: This direction IS the theme stated literally: an autonomous superbrain travelling CONSTANT into the deep-vast knowledgeable infinite. The brain never stops, never docks, never crops — it keeps its full canon voyage at all times; manufacturing is just the mind pulling a work-surface toward itself WHILE still moving through the MemoryGalaxy. The veil (dim + parallax, never freeze) is the visual proof the voyage continues behind focus: knowledge-as-stars keep drifting around the editor. The workbench floating inset, with the dimmed infinite showing on all four sides and bleeding faintly through the translucent editor, makes the surface read as summoned IN the space, never a separate flat IDE — exactly "the editor is the mind pulling up a work-surface while still voyaging." Toggling focus (Return to voyage / Enter workbench) is the mind shifting attention between travelling and making, a soft focus-pull, never a hard split — which is the assigned SUMMONED FOCUS spirit and the operator's core soul anchored faithfully.

designs.implementation: PORT-SAFE, product-only (zero ported files touched). FILES: (1) edit `frontend/src/superbrain/SuperbrainShell.jsx` — keep the single persistent `<WorkspaceCanvas/>` in `.sb-brain-stage` exactly as is; add a `.sb-veil` div rendered only when `manufacturing` (between brain stage and workbench in DOM order); keep the `.sb-workbench-stage` conditional; import a new `./manufacture.css`. Crucially do NOT add/remove any class on a node that wraps the Canvas in a way that changes mount — only toggling `.sb-shell--manufacture` on the stable outer `.sb-shell` (already the case), so the Canvas never remounts (constraint 3). (2) NEW `frontend/src/superbrain/manufacture.css` (product-only, UNLAYERED, imported after superbrain.css so it beats @layer components). SHELL LAYOUT in it: `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage{ /* deliberately NOTHING — no transform, no size — brain stays fullscreen */ }`; `.sb-veil{ position:fixed; inset:0; z-index:40; pointer-events:none; background:rgba(2,4,10,.55); backdrop-filter:blur(3px) saturate(.85); opacity:0; transition:opacity .4s ease } .sb-shell--manufacture .sb-veil{ opacity:1 }`; `.sb-shell--manufacture .sb-workbench-stage{ position:fixed; inset:76px 20px 96px; top:76px; z-index:50; padding:0; animation:workspaceRiseIn .42s cubic-bezier(.16,1,.3,1) both }`. CSS OVERRIDES BY CLASS (all scoped `.sb-shell--manufacture`): `.core-readout{display:none}`, `.hud-footer{display:none}`, `.bottom-scrim{display:none}`; `.topbar{z-index:55}`; `.command-bar{ z-index:56; width:min(680px, calc(100% - 40px)) }` (kills the 100vw escape, superbrain.css:1217); `.terminal-log{ left:auto; right:20px; bottom:88px; z-index:54; opacity:.7 }`; `.left-console, .right-console{ opacity:.5; filter:saturate(.8) }`; `.build-tag{display:none}` (optional, sanctioned). Add `@keyframes workspaceRiseIn` and tune `.wb-editor` translucency via a `.sb-shell--manufacture .wb-editor{ background:rgba(8,9,12,.74) }` override (wb-* are product files, freely editable). DOCK/CONTAINING-BLOCK TECHNIQUE USED: NONE — the deliberate absence of a transform is the whole trick. By leaving the brain fullscreen I sidestep techniques 1/2/3 entirely and with them the camera-crop, the drei-Html clipping, and the 100vw width-escape collapse to non-issues (the command-bar pin is belt-and-suspenders). NO new components needed (no rebuilt caste strip required because the real consoles aren't crammed); a product TopBar/AgentStrip remains an optional later polish, not required to ship. All overrides scoped to `.sb-shell--manufacture` so the home voyage and `?ui=superbrain` / `?ui=classic` stay byte-identical (pitfall: don't fight the home form — respected).
designs.gpuNote: Honest: this is the MOST expensive of the candidate directions on GPU because the brain renders at FULL fullscreen pixel count the entire time (a docked/smaller box would have been cheaper per constraint 5). On a 16GB laptop also running local LLM inference + Monaco + a live-preview iframe, the worst case is: fullscreen brain (full DPR + PostFX/AgX composer) + the veil's backdrop-filter blur (a fullscreen blur pass is itself non-trivial) + Monaco's own canvas + the iframe's compositing. The veil blur is the sneaky cost — a fullscreen backdrop-filter:blur recomposites the whole brain every frame. MITIGATIONS, all product-only and honest: (a) keep the veil blur LOW (3px, not 12px) or drop it to a pure opacity scrim with no blur if frames suffer — the dim alone sells "receded voyage"; (b) the operator's FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND levers stay reachable on top (LEVER E) so they can deliberately drop to a lighter tier (TIER_DPR, WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:21) while working — a sanctioned lighter manufacturing tier; (c) because the iframe sits in an opaque preview pane over the brain, that region is occluded and cheap. Net: it WILL be heavier than fullscreen-home-alone and heavier than a true small dock; it is acceptable on a 16GB machine if the veil avoids heavy blur and the operator can tier down, but I will not claim a GPU saving — there is none here; the saving was traded away for canon-perfect uncropped voyaging.
designs.risks: Honest risks: (1) GPU as above — no pixel saving; if the veil blur tanks frames it must degrade to a no-blur scrim (operator-visible quality call; never auto-degrade the BRAIN itself — FIDELITY laws). (2) READABILITY: a fullscreen voyaging brain behind a translucent editor can be visually busy/distracting while coding; the veil dim must be aggressive enough (~38%) that the workbench is the clear focus, or it feels noisy — this needs tuning in HIS browser with before/after screenshots (FIDELITY laws), and the dim amount is the single most important tunable. (3) The two drei-Html side consoles, kept visible-but-dimmed at fullscreen, sit at world x=±4.8 and at fullscreen project to the screen MARGINS — they may peek out from BEHIND the inset workbench slab on wide screens, which could read as clutter; fallback is the sanctioned `display:none` on `.left-console/.right-console` in manufacture (lab precedent <900px) if margin-peeking looks wrong — a one-line change. (4) The veil is a NEW fullscreen fixed element; I must verify its z-index sits ABOVE the Canvas/scene-layer but BELOW the topbar/command-bar (z55/56) and workbench (z50) — and that it has pointer-events:none so it never eats clicks to the live HUD beneath/above it. (5) Backdrop-filter support/perf varies; on a weak GPU it can fall back to no blur (acceptable) or cause stutter (then drop it). (6) Parity must be proven in HIS browser per the FIDELITY mandate before this ships — the brain scene is unchanged by construction (strong), but the COMPOSITION around it is new and needs his sign-off with screenshots. No constraint is violated: ported files untouched, one Canvas, brain canon + voyaging, overrides scoped to manufacturing only.

designs.name: CONTINUUM — One Knowledge-Space, No Seam
designs.oneLineConcept: There is no brain band and no IDE — there is ONE infinite MemoryGalaxy, and the workbench is a lit glass constellation drifting inside it while the brain keeps voyaging in the SAME field, flowing visibly around and behind the work-slab.
designs.brainPresentation: The single persistent <WorkspaceCanvas/> stays mounted and FULLSCREEN-shaped in manufacturing — NOT docked into a 42vh band. Technique 1 (transform-as-containing-block) is still used on .sb-brain-stage, but the sized box is the FULL viewport (inset:0 + a transform:translateZ(0) to keep being the containing block for child product chrome layering), so the fixed-FOV camera frames the canon 3.02-scale brain exactly as home — no aspect crop, no boot re-run, brain never freezes. The voyaging is untouched: same CameraDrift dolly/orbit/parallax. What changes is that the workbench slab is laid OVER the lower-centre of this same full field as a translucent glass constellation, so the brain is seen voyaging in the margins around and behind it (top, both flanks, the gaps between editor and preview). To honour the GPU budget without cropping, the brain is pushed to a deliberately lighter tier in manufacturing via the already-exposed FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE operator levers (LEVER E / QualityTierProvider TIER_DPR) — fewer pixels by lower DPR, not by a smaller box. Net: the mind is still the whole sky; the work is a window opened INTO that sky, the boldest read of the theme.
designs.hudHandling: All overrides are product-only CSS scoped under .sb-shell--manufacture (new manufacturing.css imported in SuperbrainShell.jsx, loaded after superbrain.css, NOT wrapped in @layer so it beats the layered ported rules). KEEP+REPOSITION the directive bar .command-bar (superbrain.css:1212): override the 100vw width that escapes — .sb-shell--manufacture .command-bar{width:min(680px, calc(100% - 56px)); bottom:18px} — so it docks to the field and becomes the page's always-present command line floating over the slab's bottom edge (the mind pulling up the work-surface while voyaging). HIDE the two drei-Html 3D-anchored side consoles because they project to brain world-points and cram/clip over a slab: .sb-shell--manufacture .left-console, .sb-shell--manufacture .right-console{display:none} (lab itself hides them <620px/<900px — sanctioned). HIDE the pure decoration that fights the slab: .sb-shell--manufacture .core-readout, .sb-shell--manufacture .hud-footer, .sb-shell--manufacture .bottom-scrim{display:none} (the 220px scrim would darken the field behind the slab oddly; hud-footer already drops <1100px). The .topbar (superbrain.css:238, absolute-to-experience, no 100vw) is KEPT but THINNED and lifted to read as the page's own top chrome — since the experience now fills the viewport the topbar already spans the page; override .sb-shell--manufacture .build-tag{display:none} and trim .system-summary, but keep .brand-mark, the live .status-dot + LATENCY/AUTONOMY readouts, the sovereignty .fidelity-button cluster (FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND) and the .secure-button (Supervised/HOLD/TAMPER) — all sacred levers stay reachable in place, no rebuild needed because the topbar geometry is already correct once the field is fullscreen. .terminal-log (superbrain.css:1159) is REPOSITIONED to the right gutter as a quiet activity ticker beside the slab — .sb-shell--manufacture .terminal-log{left:auto; right:22px; bottom:96px} (it is pointer-events:none ambience, perfect as the mind narrating its work in the margin). Nothing in the ported files is touched; .frame-grain is verified absent from the HUD JSX so it cannot cover the slab.
designs.workbenchPlacement: The editor + preview do NOT sit in a band below the brain — they float as a single centred glass constellation IN the field, inset from all four edges so the voyaging brain is always visible around them. The existing .sb-workbench-stage is restyled (product-only shell.css I own): instead of top:42vh;bottom:0, it becomes an inset floating region — top:78px (clear of the thinned topbar); left/right:clamp gutters (e.g. right:clamp(40px, 22vw, 360px) so the terminal-log ticker and brain show through the right margin, left:clamp(28px,8vw,140px)); bottom:84px (clear of the floated command-bar). The two .wb-pane slabs keep their glass (backdrop-filter blur + saturate already in shell.css) so the galaxy bleeds THROUGH the chrome between and behind them — editor at rgba(8,9,12,0.86) lets stars ghost through its margins, the connective 12px gap between editor and preview is a literal slot of open space showing the brain drifting behind. The slabs get a subtle parallax drift (a slow product-only CSS @keyframes translate of ±6px on .wb, 18s, prefers-reduced-motion-guarded) so the constellation itself drifts in the field rather than sitting bolted — reinforcing "a slab travelling in the same MemoryGalaxy". The preview slab stays opaque-light (#f8f9fb) as the one solid artefact (real output is concrete); the editor stays translucent (the work is still half-thought, still in the mind). Result: one seamless depth stack — stars/brain (z0) → glass slabs floating with the void showing through their seams (z2) → directive command-line + ticker as the mind's voice over it all (z3).
designs.themeFit: This is the most literal embodiment of "an autonomous superbrain travelling constant into the deep-vast knowledgeable infinite space": there is no separate IDE — the work happens INSIDE the voyage. The brain never docks to a corner and never freezes; it remains the entire sky, perpetually moving (canon CameraDrift untouched), and the act of manufacturing is the mind opening a translucent work-surface into its own infinite field. Knowledge-as-stars (the MemoryGalaxy) is visible around, between, and behind the editor — the operator literally edits while suspended in the brain's knowledge-space. The directive command-line floating at the field bottom is the mind's continuing voice; the right-gutter terminal ticker is its live cognition narrating the work. Brain flows around the work-slab exactly as the assigned direction demands: one continuous knowledge-space, no hard brain/work split, the boldest expression of his authored soul.

designs.implementation: PORT-SAFE, product-only. (1) NEW FILE frontend/src/superbrain/manufacturing.css (the override sheet), imported in SuperbrainShell.jsx AFTER './superbrain.css', NOT in any @layer. (2) SuperbrainShell.jsx (editable): keep the single persistent <WorkspaceCanvas/> exactly as-is (constraint 3) — only toggle the .sb-shell--manufacture class on the stable ancestor (already does). (3) shell.css (product-only, editable — it is workbench/shell.css, not ported): change the MANUFACTURE block so .sb-shell--manufacture .sb-brain-stage stays FULLSCREEN — inset:0; transform:translateZ(0) (containing block kept, box = viewport, so NO camera crop, the current 42vh band + box-shadow is removed); and restyle .sb-workbench-stage from a bottom band to an inset floating region (top:78px; left:clamp(28px,8vw,140px); right:clamp(40px,22vw,360px); bottom:84px; z-index:2; pointer-events:auto) plus a guarded ±6px 18s drift @keyframes on .wb. (5) NO new TopBar component is needed — because the experience stays fullscreen, the ported .topbar already spans the page correctly; we only thin it. (6) Optionally bind a deliberately lighter brain tier in manufacturing through the already-exposed FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE controls (LEVER E) — no ported edit, just selecting a lower tier on mode-enter. The single canvas is never unmounted; only classes + product CSS change. Nothing under frontend/src/superbrain/ from the port manifest is edited.
designs.gpuNote: Honest: this is the MOST expensive of the candidate directions on a 16GB laptop also running local inference + Monaco + a preview iframe, because the brain stays FULLSCREEN-shaped (it does not save pixels by shrinking the box the way a true corner-dock would). Mitigation is real but partial: (a) drop the brain to a lighter QualityTier on mode-enter via the already-exposed FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE levers (QualityTierProvider/TIER_DPR, WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:21-25) — lower DPR is the primary saving and is operator-controllable; (b) the glass slabs cover ~55-60% of the field, but the GPU still renders the full brain behind them (CSS compositing does not cull the WebGL draw), so the only honest saving is the DPR/tier reduction, NOT occlusion. The translucent editor's backdrop-filter blur is itself a compositing cost stacked over a live WebGL canvas — keep the blur modest (12-14px, already in shell.css) and avoid animating it. Net: expect this to sit at fullscreen-brain cost minus the tier reduction; if the operator's machine struggles under concurrent inference, the fallback is to accept a slightly lower FIDELITY tier in manufacturing only. A corner-dock direction would be cheaper; this one trades GPU for the boldest theme read, and that trade must be made consciously.
designs.risks: 1) GPU is the headline risk (see gpuNote) — fullscreen brain + blurred glass over live WebGL during local inference may drop frames; only the tier lever mitigates it, and a stutter would betray "never frozen". 2) READABILITY: a translucent Monaco editor over a moving starfield can hurt code legibility and contrast (accessibility) — the editor glass must stay dark/opaque enough (≥0.86 alpha, already set) that the galaxy only ghosts in the margins, never behind active code; this is a real tension between the theme and usability and may need the editor pane pushed toward near-opaque, weakening the "seamless" read. 3) MOTION SICKNESS / distraction: brain voyaging behind/around live editing plus slab parallax drift can be distracting during real work and must be prefers-reduced-motion guarded; some operators will still find it busy. 4) The .command-bar override uses position:absolute resolving to the fullscreen experience — correct here, but if the experience box ever changes shape this re-breaks; it is coupled to keeping the brain fullscreen. 5) The terminal-log right-gutter reposition relies on its pointer-events:none and ambient nature; if its internal left:calc(50%-372px) interacts oddly at narrow widths the ticker could still clip — needs a parity check in his browser. 6) This is the heaviest direction to prove FIDELITY on, since the brain renders at near-home cost — every visual change needs his-browser before/after screenshots per the FIDELITY laws. If GPU proves unacceptable, this direction cannot ship as-is and would have to fall back to a smaller-box dock, which weakens its core idea.

groundLevers: LEVER A — Product CSS overrides targeting ported class names: a new product-only stylesheet (e.g. a manufacturing.css you create and import in SuperbrainShell.jsx) loaded AFTER superbrain.css, scoped under a manufacturing root class (.sb-shell--manufacture descendant selectors) so it ONLY affects the work form and never the home/classic UIs. With equal-or-higher specificity (or :where()-free descendant selectors) you can hide (display:none), reposition (override top/left/right/bottom/position), resize (width/height/padding/font-size), and re-anchor (position:fixed to the shell) any ported HUD element by its real class: .topbar, .command-bar, .terminal-log, .left-console, .right-console, .core-readout, .bottom-scrim, .hud-footer, .secure-button, .build-tag, .console-surge, .frame-grain. This is the primary lever and is explicitly sanctioned (constraint 2).

LEVER B — The shell wrapper layout (SuperbrainShell.jsx + your shell.css): you own .sb-shell, .sb-brain-stage, .sb-workbench-stage, .sb-mode-toggle. Use the transform-containing-block technique on .sb-brain-stage to dock the brain into any box shape/position you choose (top band, side rail, corner inset), and lay out the workbench in the remaining space. Choose the box ASPECT to keep the fixed-FOV camera framing the 3.02-scale brain (prefer taller than a thin strip).

LEVER C — New product-only files/components: you may ADD product files (a product TopBar.jsx for the sovereignty row, a product AgentStrip / activity console bound to the same live data via the unmodified ported lib — cognitionBus.subscribeCognition, metricsStore.useMetric, aiosAdapter.getLastTelemetry — which are importable, just not editable). This lets you REBUILD a clean caste/telemetry strip in the workbench chrome instead of cramming the ported 350px consoles into the band.

LEVER D — Editable product app files: main.jsx (the ?ui=shell route), SuperbrainApp.jsx, SuperbrainShell.jsx, the classic App.jsx/components/lib, and vite.config.js are all product-only and editable. You can change how/where WorkspaceCanvas is mounted and wrapped (but must keep exactly ONE WorkspaceCanvas instance, constraint 3).

LEVER E — Operator sovereignty hooks already exposed: FIDELITY/SKY/SURFACE/SOUND state is owned product-side enough that you can keep these controls reachable in your product top bar; the tier system (QualityTierProvider + TIER_DPR, WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:21-25) already makes the docked brain cheaper (fewer pixels) and a lighter tier deliberately selectable for the GPU budget.
